topic: malolactic fermentation, acidity reduction, and texture

steps.label: cause
steps.text: After primary fermentation, lactic acid bacteria can convert malic acid (sharp, green-apple-like) into lactic acid (softer, milky) in a process called malolactic fermentation (MLF).

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: Malic acid has two acidic protons per molecule while lactic acid has one, so the overall acidity of the wine decreases. Simultaneously, the bacteria produce diacetyl and other by-products that contribute buttery, creamy aromatic characteristics.

steps.label: effect
steps.text: Wines that have undergone full MLF show lower perceived acidity, a rounder and creamier palate texture, and may display buttery or dairy-derived aromatic notes. These wines feel softer and more voluminous on the palate.

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Malolactic fermentation converts sharper malic acid into softer lactic acid, therefore reducing perceived acidity and adding a creamy or buttery texture. In SAT tasting terms, this manifests as medium-minus or medium acidity and a rounded, creamy mouthfeel rather than crisp freshness.

sat_relevance: Important for SAT when assessing acidity level and texture in Chardonnay or other white wines. MLF explains the move from high/crisp to medium/rounded acidity and creamy mouthfeel.

_meta.source_note: Inferred formative model - not derived from official WSET mark scheme or syllabus document
